Still some crashes

## Case 1

Variants list crashes when cancer tier key is int

To reproduce:
1. Add entry as custom term `{ "key": 6, "label": "Tier achtung", "description": "Wir haben nur ein stuhl", "label_class": "default" }`
2. Goto a cancer case
3. Classify as tier achtung
4. Goto variants list view

## Case 2

Variants list view crashes if an old evaluation term has been removed

To reproduce
1. Add a custom dismissal term to the database, `{ "key": 60, "label": "Our major issue", "description": "Major major issue", "evidence": ["pileup", "GT"] }`
2. Goto a variant for a case
3. Dismiss variant with the term "Our major issue"
4. Remove the dismissal term from the database
5. Go to case_name/variants

## Case 3

Variants list view crashes if an old evaluation term has been removed

To reproduce
1. Add a custom dismissal term to the database, `{ "key": "a-major-issue", "label": "Our major issue", "description": "Major major issue", "evidence": ["pileup", "GT"] }`
2. Goto a variant for a case
3. Dismiss variant with the term "Our major issue"
4. Remove the dismissal term from the database
5. Go to case_name/variants

IMO, there are a few requests written in the comments and also one more thing to be fixed before merging this PR.
TL;DR: We need to change the code in here(`src/ai/backend/manager/model/gql.py#1428`)
https://github.com/lablup/backend.ai/blob/40c04807d9b03040218b39ead81262ff483e79f2/src/ai/backend/manager/models/gql.py#L1415
to this
`mutation_cls = getattr(Mutations, info.field_name).type`

Considering the overall key `allowed_vfolder_hosts` are located in several policies (such as `domains`, `groups(project groups`, and `keypair_resource_policies`), I think It's quite crucial to show the overview of access in those policies easily.
In light of this, It's not surprising that users may want features for updating those access controls at once. Therefore, I decided to chain mutations so that It reduces(not completely, but some) repetitive requests to manager server. To make chained mutations, it needs [aliasing](https://atheros.ai/blog/how-to-use-graphql-aliases). Unfortunately, In current code, It regards aliasing string as one of the variables in Mutations class, which would definitely throw an error. I checked many graphql requests and It doesn't affect any mutations at all.
It would be appreciated you to apply the new code described above.

Thanks for the ping @atombrella. This dropped off my radar somehow.

I'm personally satisfied with testing here since the modified code has 100% test coverage and you added an integration test for the problem. If there's something else you'd like to test for but you're not sure the best way to do it, let me know and I can try to help.

I think we should probably structure the fix differently though. [acme.client.ClientNetwork](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/32247b3c89cb44b87f764a21e6deda9168431dec/acme/acme/client.py#L973) takes an `alg` parameter specifying the algorithm that should be used in signing JWS. The patch from #8569 ignores this attribute and instead determines the algorithm from the key type in this specific method.

I personally think we should instead do something like one of:

1. Fix where `acme.client.ClientNetwork` is created in Certbot so the right `alg` parameter is provided in this case.
2. Keeping in mind that `acme.client.ClientNetwork` is part of our public API, we could maybe deprecate the `alg` parameter and always determine the algorithm based on the key type.

What do you think? Do you have any other ideas?

If you make this change, I think you can drop commonism's commit from this PR since we'd be taking a different approach and not using that commit at all.

Let me chime in my thoughts :smile: And it might clarify lot of things

HTTPs  Requires SSL and TLS connections. This needs SSL certificate to be installed on the server etc.  Which won't be true for most dataset URLs.
 
Note that when we try opening urls from browser. Modern browsers like Firefox have **Force websites to use HTTPs over HTTP** option. So even if the url is HTTP the browser will try to open it in HTTPs.

You can try that with Kitti url. 

The harder part here is say a maintainer forgets to renew his SSL certificates. The HTTPs url won't work. While the HTTP will work fine. 

HTTP is not secure as HTTPs but as such the user can take of this browsing. As such we can't keep track of HTTP vs HTTPs urls for many datasets.


Read here

https://web.dev/enable-https/
https://www.godaddy.com/garage/enable-https-server/

Thanks for taking the time to contribute! But I don't think the brackets in this log message are incorrect.

For 3-class classification, for example, the 3 classes will have integer representations `0, 1, 2`. It's common in interval notation to use `)` to mean "excluding the final item in the interval".

So `[0, 3)` correctly refers to the set `0, 1, 2`.

See https://stackoverflow.com/a/4396303/3986677 and https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracket_(mathematics)#Intervals for more examples.

I agree that you should stick to JSON only to start with to keep things simple.  I guess most people would choose this format for nested representations.

I had a look at the draft PR.  I'm not keen on the idea of the widget class doing the heavy lifting with imports.  I think it creates significant dependencies between widgets / resources and the import process, and I think it would be better to keep these separate.

My first thought is that nested representations have been implemented effectively in DRF using Serializers.  So I wonder if this could be a useful place to start for the design.  Obviously it would be easy for users to understand this if they are familiar with DRF, and if we plug-in their implementation then we benefit from the stability of their Serializer implementation.

I expect using DRF serializers would have to be an optional plugin in order to maintain backwards compatibility.  This might involve a significant re-write of our existing code.  I have not thought about it in much depth yet.

So I think this would be a great feature, but it looks like it could be a sizeable chunk of work.  Is there anything else out there that imports / exports nested data?

This change is correct. C++20 6.4.7 [basic.scope.class] says:

> A name N used in a class S shall refer to the same declaration in its context and when re-evaluated in the completed scope of S. No diagnostic is required for a violation of this rule.

The name `task` changes meaning in this class scope from being the type `tbb::task` to being the member function `task()`. In C++23 this will be a required diagnostic.

The fix it to use the name `tbb::task` instead, which is unambiguous.

Two odd things I noticed, not sure what the fix is.

> Is there a reason why we are preferring Werkzeug over SimpleHTTPServer?

So, the Python stdlib has two related things. First is `http.server`,
which was called `SimpleHTTPServer` in Python 2. This is a static server
that only serves from disk. Great for lots of cases, but not useful here
since we need to serve the contents of a zip file. We could extract it
and serve from disk, but then you have to re-extract each time the
contents change, so you have to clean up a tempdir or keep some state
somewhere. That sounds more complicated than this.

The other is `wsgiref`, which provides “a simple HTTP server” that is
not the same as `SimpleHTTPServer`: namely, `wsgiref.simple_server`. We
could definitely use this, and I did so in bazelbuild/rules_rust#475 to
avoid pulling in a Werkzeug dep. But we already have Werkzeug, so I
thought that this was just a bit simpler.

At least for me, our `@org_pocoo_werkzeug` dep doesn’t actually seem to
be linking right, in that when I run this server outside of a virtualenv
it can’t find `werkzeug`. I judged this fine since we all obviously use
TensorBoard virtualenvs, anyway. But it is a reasonable point in favor
of just using the standard library.

Would you prefer that I avoid Werkzeug for the app and the server? You
can see what that’d look like here:
<https://github.com/bazelbuild/rules_rust/pull/475/files#diff-806deb54546ccb18b46d212494d862925348c2e9d939c9e88cbf3b79e81ec4a1R1>

This looks like a great start. I have a few thoughts about the design.

If you added a `controller` section to [kubernetes.yaml](https://github.com/dask/dask-kubernetes/blob/main/dask_kubernetes/kubernetes.yaml) we could use the Dask config system instead of using global constants.

```yaml
kubernetes:
  ...
  controller:
    worker-allocation:
      batch-size: null
      delay: null
```

Then you can use the environment variables `DASK_KUBERNETES__CONTROLLER__WORKER_ALLOCATION__BATCH_SIZE` and `DASK_KUBERNETES__CONTROLLER__WORKER_ALLOCATION__DELAY` in the chart to set them and you can access the values in Python.

```python
import dask.config

...

dask.config.get("kubernetes.controller.worker-allocation.batch-size")
dask.config.get("kubernetes.controller.worker-allocation.delay")
```

(You might find [this utility](https://docs.dask.org/en/latest/configuration.html#conversion-utility) useful for converting between the different formats.)

I'm also keen to let `kopf` help us as much as possible. When batching we effectively want to call the scale workers handler but only scale up by the max batch size, then we want to wait for some period of time and then call the scale workers handler again until we have eventually scaled to our desired number. There is a way to do this by raising exceptions in `kopf`, see my comment below for more information.

Thanks for tackling this @viren-nadkarni ! 👍 Great catch on removal of `DEFAULT_REGION` - this dates back to ancient times when LS was not fully multi-region capable yet. :)

Regarding removal of `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`/`AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`, not sure if this is a great user experience - so far, the premise was always that `awslocal` should ideally work out of the box, using sensible defaults where needed. I can see that this change could create some headache and break quite some existing pipelines out there. 🤔 (At least we shouldn't introduce it with a patch/minor relase imo.)
```
$ awslocal s3 mb s3://foo
make_bucket failed: s3://foo Unable to locate credentials
```

Could we maybe attempt to create a boto3 client, which will go through the credentials resolver chain, and then selectively only set individual environment variable defaults if the client cannot be created? Something like the following (not fully fleshed out, just conceptually):
```
try:
    boto3.client("sts").get_caller_identity()
except botocore.exceptions.NoCredentialsError:
    env_dict["AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID"] = "test"
    env_dict["AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY"] = "test"

# optionally, we could also add a fallback for the region:
try:
    boto3.client("sqs").list_queues()
except botocore.exceptions.NoRegionError:
    env_dict["AWS_DEFAULT_REGION"] = "..."
```

This will add a tiny fraction to the runtime of `awslocal` commands, but I think it would make for a better user experience. Also, this way we could still make use of profiles and `~/.aws/config`. If we're concerned about the additional runtime overhead, we could make the checks configurable (with an option to switch off the setting of defaults).

Thoughts?

Will try to restate my previous review at a higher level. Ultimately, I'm worried that it's too difficult to reason about this interface, because it's too difficult to really understand the details right now. I think there are really 2 approaches to making it more understandable:
1. Flush out the exact semantics of the API, the meaning of the fields, and the interaction/error model with the relevant systems we interact with.
2. Make sure everyone is on the same page about the principals of the design (ideally by stating them) so that the exact semantics and failure model are well defined.

(2) is probably more productive. 

In particular, I think we're close to following a few principals that would give us a safe system (and it was my understanding that we were originally attempting to follow them).

========= These 2 comments apply both to `autoscaler_state_service.proto` and `instance_manager.proto` ==============

* Abstraction: The autoscaler should only know relevant parts of the scheduler state, but not necessarily details about how it evolves. Practically, the main fix here is would be to avoid guaranteeing implementation details about the version (like a "monotonically increasing ..."). In fact we may even find `int64` to be a limiting implementation detail since versioning could be per-raylet (and we've discussed getting reports directly from raylet before). Concretely, I believe changing the version type to `bytes` and changing the guarantee to "If 2 states have the same version, they are equivalent/interchangeable for autoscaling purposes" would fix the issue.
* E2e principle: The autoscaler operates at a higher layer than the cluster. Node launching functionality can and must be implemented at the autoscaler layer therefore the scheduler interface shouldn't interpret it.

========== This final comment primarily applies to instance_manager.proto =========

* Optimistic concurrency control: The instance manager is quite close to relying on optimistic concurrency control to ensure that autoscaler decisions are carried out safely (e.g. on the expected state). For this to work, we need to check that the instance manager state hasn't changed. Again, concretely one way to achieve this would be by defining a "version" concept (which can be typed as `bytes`) in which any two states with the same "version" are equivalent for autoscaling purposes. If the versions are different, we don't need to define the behavior if the versions are different, so long as the version changes less frequently than the decision making time.
* Simplicity: Perhaps this is a matter of taste, but I prefer it when interfaces are as simple as possible, therefore, it would be nice to not include fields if we don't know their purpose. Relying on other fields without defining the principles has the potential to be dangerous/unsafe.

I have concerns regarding this feature:
- SRP clients will detect `network data` change and switch to another server if the BR is off for a few minutes. So, we already have some form of auto-switching feature.  
- If TREL is enabled on BRs, reachability to one BR basically means reachability to all BRs. Which means SRP clients won't have many reachability issues with BRs.
- If a SRP client switches to another server, the origianl server, if running, still has the SRP services. The SRP client may fail to register on the new server due to probing failures.
- Occasional bust of channel failures may cause registration failures. In such case, it's not useful to switch to another server. SRP clients should keep using the current SRP server in such case. 

It seems unclear to me what's the problem that switching is trying to solve.

Just one question 😄

I haven't looked much at the code in particular but  I did notice that there is only one root `.gitignore` file in the eyes of `get_sources`. This is a problem since files outside of the scope (or should I say folder) of a `.gitignore` file would still be subject to its exclude rules.

Let's say we have a directory structure like this: 
```
.
├── a
│   ├── a.py
│   ├── b.py
│   └── .gitignore
├── a.py
├── b.py
├── c.py
└── .gitignore
```

And with `./.gitignore` containing `c.py` and `./a/.gitignore` containing `b.py`. 

I would expect that only `./c.py` and `./a/b.py` would be ignored by Git and Black. This is true with Git, but not with Black. Black also ignores the root level `b.py` file because the nested `.gitignore`'s contents was added to the "root" `.gitignore` that all files and directories Black recursively are subject to.

```console
(black) ichard26@acer-ubuntu:~/programming/oss/black$ gh pr checkout 1750
(black) ichard26@acer-ubuntu:~/programming/testing/gitignore$ git status
On branch master
Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
	a.py
	a/a.py
	b.py

nothing added to commit but untracked files present (use "git add" to track)
(black) ichard26@acer-ubuntu:~/programming/testing/gitignore$ black .  --check -v
.git ignored: matches the --exclude regular expression
c.py ignored: matches the .gitignore file content
b.py ignored: matches the .gitignore file content
a/b.py ignored: matches the .gitignore file content
/home/ichard26/programming/testing/gitignore/a/a.py already well formatted, good job.
/home/ichard26/programming/testing/gitignore/a.py already well formatted, good job.
All done! ✨ 🍰 ✨
2 files would be left unchanged.
```

Perhaps make `gen_python_files` only search through one directory and then return the non-excluded files and subdirectories it found. So similar to `os.walk`. And then on every call to `gen_python_files` in `get_sources` a "`.gitignore` file" is created that makes sense for the current directory it's searching. To make this work you would also have to keep track of the all different `.gitignore` files and their locations.

*I'm sorry if that didn't make any sense at all, I'm not great at explain ideas clearly... Anyway, it's just a suggestion to how you could fix this issue. You probably can think of something better :D

I like where this is going.

Let me reconstruct the narrative here.

So Pulumi has an important type PropertyValue that:

- is the basis of $lang - engine RegisterResource protocol

- is the basis of the diff algo on resources

- figures in state files

The grammar of these is defined by resource.PropertyValue constructors and soon-to-be-added matching JSON schema.

These representations figure in the PR:

- native strongly-typed PropertyValue representation
- JSON-like representation (parsed as `map[string]interface{}` in Go but equivalently serializable to JSON)
- protobuf pb.Struct representation

The PR tests the turnaround properties on these representations.

The representations not covered pertain to no-Go language SDKs; essentially every supported  $lang talks the pb.Struct representation.

I was a bit confused why we need to define rapid generators over JSON and protobuf repr's if we believe these values only ever originate from the strongly-typed representation but it makes sense if we need to double-check the wire repr or we plant to evolve the wire repr somewhat separately and not just as a rigid function fo the strongly-typed repr.

One high-level thing I'd suggest is that we could actually create a new function in summary_v2.py that's called `histogram_v3()`, and then we can stage these changes in a way that affects that function only, which would make it easier to get them landed in smaller PRs without worrying about it being a breaking change yet for existing `tf.summary.histogram()` users (since there are some other places in TensorBoard that we probably should update before releasing the format change itself, as the design doc describes).

My suggestion would be to start out with a separate change in which we create `histogram_v3()` as basically a copy of `histogram()` but without all the special handling for name scopes and TPU outside compilation.  Essentially `histogram_v3` would look like rolling back `histogram` to the simple version we had before all the TPU issues: https://cs.opensource.google/tensorflow/tensorboard/+/master:tensorboard/plugins/histogram/summary_v2.py;l=69-79;drc=fdeeb395deaa953d2e5e6375c418a84feb05800b

I'd then re-apply this one later change to use LazyTensorCreator, since that's useful even aside from the TPU issues: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/pull/2899

I'd also create a copy of the bucketing helper function, `_buckets_v3()` which would be used by `histogram_v3()`.

Once we have that set up, we can easily land changes like this that affect only `_buckets_v3()` and have a separate set of tests, etc.  Once it's working and we've vetted it on TPU, we can cut over the public API symbol from the v2 to the v3 function.  How does that sound?

Though your code on the PR looks good, I'm skeptical merging this for a few reasons:

I'm a strong :-1: on this for the QtPy 2.0 release, because:

* We've tested and maintained compatibility all the way up to the release, so it doesn't really make sense to suddenly drop it now and make it impossible for Python 3.6 users to install, despite it being fully functional
* There isn't a compelling benefit to do so immediately; no compat code was simplified/removed and it didn't simplify the CI setup at all (at least as of the current state of the PR); running `pyupgrade --py37-plus` over the full codebase, only one line was changed, and that one only trivially.
* It means all of our tens of thousands of downstream dependencies will either have to drop Python 3.6 support or not upgrade to QtPy 2.0 until they do, and there will be never be a supported combination of Qt6 and Python 3.6
* All of our supported bindings (Pyside2, Pyside6, PyQt5, PyQt6) all still support Python 3.6, so it would be premature to drop it before any of them do
* Checking the [PyPI download stats](https://pypistats.org/packages/qtpy), besides the large spike for 3.7 on Linux (likely driven by CI/automation systems), over the past six months Python 3.6 was a strong number 3 behind Python 3.8 in popularity for QtPy downloads, occasionally even exceeding it.

Furthermore, if we do decide to merge this, there are several specific additional items that need to be investigated/confirmed, and this blocks work on #269 until it is closed or resolved since it touches the same lines.

I strongly recommend that we close this PR for now, retain the existing Python 3.6 support in QtPy 2.0.0, and then drop it in a future 2.1.0 or a later minor version after the EoL, subject to revision if PyQt/PySide dropping support or a non-trivial Py36-only bug crops up that I'm not able to easily solve. I'm happy to commit to taking care of this myself, including verifying all the skips, at any point in QtPy 2.1.0 or later and after the EoL next month.

This is really exciting! I don't understand the graph code in detail, so my review is very naive, though.

Instead of a detailed code review, I tried it with the `run` and `test` goals: https://gist.github.com/huonw/f79d06824fd53e2dd192d3338ad270d9. It generally worked as I'd expect, including handling codegen and target selection nicely, except for the test runs: actions 8 and 9.

Run `PANTS_SOURCE=... pants run :runner`:

| # | action | should restart `:runner`? | actually restart? | pass |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | edit `runner.py` | :white_check_mark:  | :white_check_mark:  | :white_check_mark: | 
| 2 | edit `dep.py` | :white_check_mark:  | :white_check_mark:  | :white_check_mark:  |
| 3 | edit the comment in `generator.py` (no output change) | :x: | :x: (`pants.log` implies the generator is rerun ✅ ) | :white_check_mark:  |
| 4 | edit the print in `generator.py` (output changes) | :white_check_mark: | :white_check_mark: | :white_check_mark: 
| 5 | edit `unrelated.py` | :x: | :x:  | :white_check_mark: |
| 6 | edit `:runner` in `BUILD` (e.g. adding tags) | :white_check_mark:* | :white_check_mark: | :white_check_mark: |
| 7 | edit other parts of `BUILD`, e.g. comments | :x: | :x: | :white_check_mark: |

\* For the specific case of editing tags, I don't think this could ever change the runtime behaviour(?), so arguably no restart would be better behaviour... but that seems like a detail specific to only a few fields, and doesn't apply to all situations (e.g. `--filter-tag-regex` target filtering means tag changes can change the behaviour for other goals, as tested below).

Run `PANTS_SOURCE=... pants test ::`, and while it is running:

| # |action | should restart? | actually restart? | pass |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | edit `test_fast.py` | only `test_fast.py` | both `test_fast.py` and `test_slow.py` | :x: |
| 9 | edit `test_slow.py` | only `test_slow.py` | both `test_fast.py` and `test_slow.py` | :x: |

Run `PANTS_SOURCE=... pants test --filter-tag-regex=some_tag ::`, and while it is running:

| # | action | expected behaviour | actual behaviour | pass |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | add `some_tag` to `test_fast.py` | `test_fast.py` is run, `test_slow.py` continues running | `test_fast.py` is run (`test_slow.py` also restarts, covered by 8) | :white_check_mark: |
| 11 | remove `some_tag` from `test_slow.py` | `test_slow.py` is interrupted and the test run edits immediately | as expected | :white_check_mark: |

I have 2 follow-up questions / comments, which we can address (or not) in other PRs:

- As discussed offline, JIT "isn't supported" for the datapoints on these dispatchers. What we mean by "not supported" is that the datapoints will be treated as a pure Tensors, and the output will be a Tensor too. I assume this can lead to unexpected results and confusion, e.g.:

    ```py
    mask = Mask(...)
    out = T(mask)
    # - unaware user expects a Mask but gets a Tensor
    # - `T` treats mask as an image, not as a mask!
    #    Might result in different behaviour (very bad) or confusing error message?
    ```

    so: **should we fail loudly** with a good error message in the disaptchers when `is_scripting()` is True and `is_simple_tensor()` is False?


- Side note: should we be more lenient with `mypy` checks in the prototype area?
We introduced strict checks in https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/4513 (and I approved the PR, so I'll concede I'm sort of rebutting myself here).
But the the prototype area is where we want to be able to move fast, and try potentially crazy things without having to worry too much about type checks / docs / super clean tests. In this specific case, the introduction of `warn_return_any` could have been the reason for these convoluted checks in the first place which complicates the code. Perhaps we may want to avoid to relax the mypy requirements in order to experiment faster in the prototype area?

```json
{
   "host": "192.168.1.5",
   "mac": "aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff",
   "type": 0,
   "hardware": "non_mystrom",
   "is_child": false,
   "mystrom_registered": false,
   "mystrom_online": false,
   "restarted":false
}
```

Hello @hemanth-silabs 

Thank you for this contribution, it's an important step towards more secure products! Good work!

I have an overall question about architecture. Right now the code is sliced with ifdefs in many places to choose between PSA API and mbedTLS API. I think this is good for the first phase. We might want to implement some unions and common class to gather both raw key and reference together.

For later (not in this PR).. Haven't you considered using only the PSA API (whenever possible)? As far as I understand, we can update the mbedTLS version to one that supports PSA API (e.g. 2.25 from TF-M 1.3) - I have it done on a branch and can contribute. Since for non-TF-M type of devices, PSA API implementation translates code to mbedTLS API under the hood anyway. For example the `psa_hash_update` will call `mbedtls_sha256_update_ret` - [code](https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbedtls/blob/v2.25.0/library/psa_crypto.c#L2655). The only caveat is that for such non-TF-M devices, we would need to implement ITS API for the key-by-reference feature. As an additional argument, I believe mbedTLS will not have "old" mbedTLS API in due time anyway.

One last small change, otherwise this looks good!

TL;DR I don't see how this patch improves anything we specifically care about, it only seems to make the maintenance burden worse if anything. So I'm strongly opposed to adding the check but updates to `pyproject.toml` are fine.

PEP 517 build front-end uses `pyproject.toml` to populate the build env. When `setuptools` can be specified uncapped, in practice, the front-ends (like pip) will pull in the very latest version of it. So we are covered here.

Invoking `setuptools` directly is deprecated. Here's why https://blog.ganssle.io/articles/2021/10/setup-py-deprecated.html.
And also `distutils` has been deprecated for quite a while now so importing it is rather discouraged.

So here's my request:
1) Drop the changes from `setup.py`
2) Move the `[build-system]` to the top of `pyproject.toml`
3) Update the change note to reflect this and use the past tense there (you may find a more comprehensive guide in `CHANGES/README.rst`
